retrieved_docs.title: Trichinella spiralis Infection(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Classic trichinellosis symptoms usually occur in 2 weeks after consumption of raw or undercooked meat and can last up to 8 weeks. Symptoms include fevers, chills, myalgias, periorbital or facial edema, weakness, and fatigue. Reports also exist of prolonged diarrhea. [9] . Other common symptoms are conjunctivitis and subconjunctival hemorrhages seen in about 50% of patients. [10] Splinter hemorrhages on nailbeds (subungual) and retinal hemorrhages can also occur. The rash is most commonly in the form of urticaria but can present with splinter hemorrhage and petechiae. A key finding is palpebral edema which is often associated with proptosis and chemosis. Other less common manifestations include a headache, cough, rash, headache, dyspnea, and dysphagia. Hepatomegaly can also occur occasionally. [11] Severe complications include myocarditis, life-threatening arrhythmias, meningitis, encephalitis, respiratory myositis, secondary bacterial pneumonia, hematuria, and renal failure. [12] [13] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Muscle Rupture -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Papillary muscle rupture is a rare but potentially fatal complication, typically occurring after myocardial infarction or due to infective endocarditis. Acute rupture often results in severe mitral valve regurgitation, leading to acute life-threatening cardiogenic shock and pulmonary edema. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Hyperthermia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications of malignant hyperthermia include: Brain damage Cardiac arrest Death Heart failure Internal bleeding Kidney failure Pulmonary edema Skeletal muscle degeneration

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Muscle Rupture -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Papillary muscle rupture is a rare but potentially fatal complication that typically follows a myocardial infarction or occurs secondary to infective endocarditis. Acute rupture frequently results in severe mitral valve regurgitation, acute life-threatening cardiogenic shock, and pulmonary edema. Papillary muscle dysfunction leads to regurgitation of blood through the valves, causing the backward flow of blood and, potentially, left- or right-sided heart failure.

retrieved_docs.title: Boutonneuse Fever -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, most outcomes follow a benign clinical course. However, more serious disease manifestations can occur more frequently in certain at-risk populations. These include myocarditis, atrial fibrillation, retinopathy, retinal vasculitis, cerebral infarct, meningoencephalitis, sensorineural hearing loss, acute quadriplegia secondary to an axonal polyneuropathy, splenic rupture, and the presence of hemophagocytic syndrome. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Muscle Rupture -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Papillary muscle rupture is a rare but severe mechanical complication that can occur following an acute myocardial infarction, affecting 0.07% to 0.26% of patients. Despite the condition's rarity, papillary muscle rupture contributes to 5% of postmyocardial infarction mortality. [9] Papillary muscle rupture leads to severe mitral valve regurgitation, often resulting in cardiogenic shock and pulmonary edema, necessitating immediate medical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Muscle Rupture -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors for papillary muscle rupture include advanced age, female sex, a history of heart failure, delayed treatment after myocardial infarction, and chronic kidney disease. The mortality rate for papillary muscle rupture can reach up to 75% within the first 24 hours when treated with medical therapy alone and without surgical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Muscle Rupture -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A study involving 22 patients reported a perioperative mortality rate of 27% and overall survival of 47% at 7 years. Another study involving 55 patients indicated an overall operative mortality rate of 24%. The mortality rate was notably higher in patients who did not undergo coronary revascularization, with a rate of 39% compared to 9% in individuals who received the procedure. In addition, in a study involving 54 patients, the 10-year survival rate was reported to be 35%, and the 10-year heart failure-free rate was 23%.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Myocardial Infarction -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Several complications may result from myocardial infarction. Arrhythmias are possible at any point in the course of MI but are common early in the course, within the first three days. It is possible to develop reinfarction or extension of infarction. Mechanical complications are also possible and may include ventricular free wall rupture, ventricular septal rupture, papillary muscle rupture, acute mitral valve regurgitation, and ventricular aneurysms. These carry significant morbidity and mortality. Heart failure and cardiogenic shock are possible. There is an increased risk of embolic phenomena, which can lead to peripheral ischemia or stroke. Pericarditis, which may be early or late, can also occur. Late-onset pericarditis is also known as Dressler syndrome or post-cardiac injury syndrome and is an autoimmune response. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1522
retrieved_docs.content: The medical taxonomy places “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” as a distinct category that includes the specific manifestation labeled “غشي.” The term “Symptoms” is described as closely related and possibly synonymous with “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating substantial overlap in meaning. Moreover, the broader grouping “diseases and injuries” is identified as the parent of “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” positioning these vague clinical signs and symptoms as subcomponents within the larger domain of diseases and injuries. This hierarchical and synonymous relationship helps clinicians organize and interpret poorly defined clinical presentations within the overall framework of disease and injury.
